data.title: Jet fuel the most immediate problem from Iran war – Paul Sankey
data.description:
Paul Sankey, president of Sankey Research, warned that jet fuel has become the “most immediate problem” stemming from the ongoing Iran war, with prices surging to $200 per barrel even as crude oil remains surprisingly under $100.

In an interview with CNBC, Sankey noted that the Strait of Hormuz closure has now exceeded 50 days, creating cascading supply chain disruptions across global energy markets.

The International Energy Agency expects the jet fuel shortage to spread into Europe and cause market volatility for up to two years. While the U.S. is in relatively good shape, Asia is facing severe challenges due to its dependence on disrupted supply routes.

“It’s been quite surprising for us given the scale of the crisis,” Sankey said of the market’s muted reaction to crude prices despite the massive disruptions.

Australia has emerged as a critical flashpoint in the crisis. Sankey described the country as already experiencing “a real crisis” because it holds only about 35 days of emergency oil stocks compared to the recommended 90 days.

The nation’s position at the end of the supply chain, dependent on Korea and Singapore for oil products, has left it particularly vulnerable.

European travelers face a difficult summer ahead as jet fuel demand typically rises by 40% during the season. With fuel representing roughly 30% of a plane ticket’s cost, Sankey predicted that “a lot of holidays are going to be canceled,” with charter flights likely to be cut first as airlines struggle to meet demand.

The total oil supply disruption currently ranges between 10M-20M barrels per day, with an emerging threat from the Houthis potentially putting an additional 4M-7M barrels of Saudi Arabian deliveries at risk in the Red Sea.

“The outage is absolutely staggering,” Sankey said.

Looking ahead, Sankey emphasized that countries would need to dramatically improve their emergency oil inventory planning after years of ignoring international recommendations.

While a “flood of oil” will eventually hit markets once the straits reopen, the crisis has exposed critical vulnerabilities that will require better global emergency preparedness.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) are on the rise at $88, while Brent (CO1:COM) also rose to $95 per barrel.

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Airline ETFs: (JETS).

data.title: Bar Harbor Bankshares dividend preview: analysts expects ~1.56% increase this quarter
data.description:
Bar Harbor Bankshares (BHB) is expected to announce a dividend increase this April, which would keep its 22-year streak of consecutive dividend hikes going.

Based on historical trends, analysts are expecting a consensus annual dividend to move up to about ~$1.30 per share, which works out to a quarterly dividend of around ~$0.325. This represents an increase of ~1.56% from the current ~$0.32 per share payout.

The last increase came in April 2025, when the dividend went up from ~$0.30 to ~$0.32, a stronger jump of ~6.7%. Since then, the company has held steady at ~$0.32 per share, including the January 2026 payout.

At the current dividend levels, the stock offers an annual dividend yield of ~3.67%. Over the last five years, dividends have grown ~7.78% annually, while the payout ratio sits at ~38.65%. 

On dividends quality metrics, it scores D+ for safety, D+ for growth, B for yield, and A for consistency. Investors should also keep an eye on the upcoming Q1 2026 earnings results, which are expected to be announced soon.

data.title: Broadcom shares slip after nine-session winning streak
data.description:
Broadcom (AVGO) shares snapped nine straight sessions of gains on Monday, as the stock was 2.3% lower at $397.30.

The semiconductor and infrastructure software solutions company gained nearly 22% in the preceding nine sessions. So far this year, the stock has added over 16%, compared to the 4% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index (SP500).

AVGO is up 24% over the past one month.

Most recently, Broadcom entered into a three-year partnership with Meta Platforms (META) to help the company develop its artificial intelligence chips and deploy them into data centers.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, AVGO has a Hold rating with a score of 3.5 out of 5. The company received A+ in the prospect of profitability and growth, while it received F in the valuation factor.

Seeking Alpha analysts are positive and see the stock as a Buy.

Turning to the Wall Street, 44 analysts have given the stock a Buy or above rating. Three gave the stock hold recommendation, while none gave Sell rating to AVGO.

 

“Despite a premium valuation, Broadcom offers significant upside as earnings scale rapidly, with potential for substantial re-rating as AI demand continues accelerating through 2027,” pointed out a recent Seeking Alpha analysis.

data.title: Markets are underestimating Iran – Rapidan Energy Group CEO
data.description:
Scott Modell, CEO of Rapidan Energy Group, is warning that markets are significantly underestimating Iran’s resilience and the difficulty of reaching a diplomatic resolution in the ongoing standoff.

In an interview with CNBC, Modell expressed skepticism about optimistic statements from the administration, noting that fundamental realities suggest a deal remains far out of reach.

The ongoing maritime blockade has emerged as the central obstacle to any progress in negotiations. Modell explained that as long as Iran’s ships are unable to move and are being seized, the Iranians view this as “a showstopper” and an escalation that violates the ceasefire.

“As long as there is a blockade in place, the Iranians are not going to allow talks,” he said, adding that there’s a good chance they won’t show up to scheduled discussions.

Modell emphasized that investors may be underestimating Iran’s internal preparation for withstanding prolonged pressure. According to his assessment, Iran believes it has built in several months of reserves and is willing to wait out the international community.

The market’s tendency to react quickly to positive news stands in stark contrast to the reality on the ground, where both sides engaged in “stress testing” over the weekend.

Modell noted that the reescalation was “totally in line” with Rapidan’s thinking that the two sides remain far apart. He added that his firm is consistently surprised by how quickly markets respond to rosy statements suggesting the conflict is over.

Iran has established specific red lines that complicate any potential framework for resolution. Modell pointed out that the Trump administration previously rejected a memorandum of understanding in Islamabad that would have allowed for extended talks and sequenced negotiations. Iran is unlikely to agree to any partial deal while the blockade persists.

Looking ahead, Modell offered a sobering assessment of the near-term outlook.

A deal is going to be “really, really difficult to get, certainly in the next couple of days to weeks,” he said.

For energy markets, underestimating Iranian resolve remains a primary risk that investors would be wise to consider.

data.title: Commerzbank formally rejects UniCredit's approach
data.description:
Commerzbank (CRZBF) (CRZBY) has formally rejected UniCredit's (UNCFF) (UNCRY) approach, the German lender said in a statement on Monday.

Last month, the Italian bank disclosed a €35B offer to increase its stake in Commerzbank to over 30%.

UniCredit CEO Andrea Orcel had reportedly said the bid is designed to allow the bank to boost its stake to over 30% without triggering a requirement to make an offer for complete ownership.

Orcel was set to hold a conference call at 9.00 AM CET today. UniCredit was set to outline the details ​of "an approach to value ​creation" for Commerzbank, adding that it believed the ​German lender is insufficiently prepared for future challenges and is overly ​focused on short-term ​delivery.

In the latest statement, Commerzbank said it has taken note of the Italian lender's presentation.

"Commerzbank has tried to engage constructively with UniCredit, but no concrete combination plan for a mutually agreeable value-accretive transaction nor any reliable indications for a commensurate control premium from UniCredit have been put forward," said the statement.

"What UniCredit has presented today is not a value-creating business combination—it is a stand-alone restructuring proposal that has to be evaluated against the existing strategy of Commerzbank that delivers real, reliable value with limited execution risk," said the German bank's CEO, Bettina Orlopp.

Commerzbank is set to present its updated financial targets and strategy through 2030 on May 8. The bank will set out a comprehensive response to UniCredit's specific claims after the Italian lender has published the full details of its announced takeover offer.

data.title: Cliffs expects Q2 selling prices up about $60 a ton as it targets $425M idled property cash receipts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "The first quarter of 2026 was the beginning of a sustained improvement progression that will continue through the rest of the year" (Chairman, President & CEO Lourenco Goncalves), while adding that "Q1 results could be better... if not for a couple of one-timers," with "the impact of the spiking on energy costs" the most significant.

  • "Our order book is full and the automotive OEMs are booking more and more steel from Cliffs" (CEO Goncalves), alongside "tight" production schedules and lead times moving out; he said pricing realization has shifted: "Today, that lag is closer to 2 months."

  • "Steel imports into the United States are at their lowest levels, since 2009" (CEO Goncalves), who said, "Section 232 works," and highlighted "recent changes in how derivative products tariffs are being enforced" including "Distribution transformers were added."

  • "In my long career in this business, I have never seen so much momentum in substituting aluminum with steel" (CEO Goncalves), extending beyond autos into "building products, appliances and truck and trailer sectors," and said Cliffs is demonstrating forming steel in equipment "previously utilized exclusive for aluminum."

  • "In February, Cleveland-Cliffs received... Toyota, the Toyota Quality Excellence Award" (CEO Goncalves), saying it confirms quality and execution against Toyota’s standards.

  • "At Burns Harbor, we are idling our smaller plate mill" and "idling the Gary Plate finishing line" (CEO Goncalves), while stating "There will be no loss in overall steel production or layoffs" due to backfilling roles from retiree attrition.

  • "We have also partnered with a leading and prominent AI provider" (CEO Goncalves) to embed AI into production planning and order entry, and added, "We expect to make a full announcement... in the next few weeks."

  • "Our adjusted EBITDA in the quarter was $95 million" (Executive VP & CFO Celso Goncalves), and he said the quarter’s top-line recovery included "shipments totaled just over 4.1 million tons."

Outlook

  • "Q2 shipments are expected to improve from Q1 and remain above that 4.1 million ton mark" (CFO Goncalves), and he framed the trajectory as "Q2 is going to be much better than Q1 and Q3 should be much better than Q2."

  • "Selling prices are expected to be up about $60 a ton from Q1 to Q2" (CFO Goncalves), with the realization lag described as extending "closer to 2 months" as the order book fills.

  • "Our Q2 costs should tick up another $15 per ton higher" (CFO Goncalves), citing higher scrap and fuel and "some scheduled outages in Q2," before costs "falling meaningfully in the back half of the year."

  • "All of our other full year expectations, including volume, CapEx and SG&A remain in line with prior guidance" (CFO Goncalves).

Financial results

  • "First quarter shipments totaled just over 4.1 million tons" (CFO Goncalves), who also reported "Average selling prices increased by $68 per ton from a year ago and sequentially by $55 per ton during the quarter," while noting results were "slightly below our original estimate" due to longer-than-anticipated contractual lags.

  • "The energy spike drove an $80 million negative impact to EBITDA on Q1 relative to historical expectations" (CFO Goncalves), tied to natural gas lock timing plus "electricity and industrial gases."

  • "First quarter free cash flow was negative as expected" (CFO Goncalves), attributing it "primarily due to working capital timing," and said receivables built as "shipments accelerated into March" with pricing higher.

  • "Our $425 million cash receipt expectation from idled property sales remains on target" (CFO Goncalves), adding "2 more properties" went under contract since the prior update and liquidity remained "above $3 billion."

Q&A

  • Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley: Asked about Q2 pricing and the remaining impact of the ArcelorMittal Calvert slab tail; Chairman, President & CEO Goncalves replied, "It was 175,000 tons of slabs... but it's over. It's done," and CFO Goncalves added, "Selling prices are expected to be up about $60 a ton from Q1 to Q2" and outlined contract mix including "about 43%" fixed and "15%" Stelco spot.

  • Nick Giles, B. Riley Securities: Asked about working capital unwind and whether POSCO deal terms are firming; CFO Goncalves said the Q1 working capital build was "about $130 million" and Q2 should see "a slight release," while CEO Goncalves said on POSCO, "we are no longer in a hurry" and want "a deal that is accretive for our shareholders."

  • Martin Englert, Seaport Research: Asked about diesel exposure/hedging and aluminum-to-steel switching; CFO Goncalves said, "We don't hedge diesel anymore" and described diesel as "about a $50 million annual impact" ("about $6 per ton"), while CEO Goncalves said substitution is "happening as we speak" and noted, "We brought back the EGL line... at New Carlyle."

  • Nicklaus Cash, Goldman Sachs: Asked about slab math and confidence in Q3 cost drop; CFO Goncalves said, "your math is reasonable" with offsets (scrap and energy) and pointed to Q2 outages versus Q3 being "a very outage light quarter" with utilization-driven "cost dilution."

  • Albert Realini, Jefferies: Asked about trade-offs if POSCO doesn’t materialize versus selling higher-value assets; CEO Goncalves responded, "I don't like playing hypothetical scenarios," adding "shipments continue to go more towards the 16.5 million to 17 million tons for the year" and said HBI supports higher production.

  • Lawson Winder, BofA: Pressed on Q2 cost components and land sale timing; CFO Goncalves cited outages, richer auto mix, and "war-related costs related to diesel and freight," and CEO Goncalves said asset sale contracts are progressing and, on cadence, later guided, "Let's put $50 million in Q2 and $100 million in Q3 with the remainder in Q4."

  • Timna Tanners, Wells Fargo: Asked about plate actions and electrical steel; CEO Goncalves said the idled plate mill was tied to "one specific client" and stated, "the 160-inch mill is now fully utilized," while adding EVs are "the biggest utilization of oriented electrical steels" and said for grain-oriented electrical steels, "there is only one company... that's Cliffs."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical, focusing on bridge items (pricing lag, working capital, cost walk, POSCO optionality), with pointed prompts like "What gives you confidence" (Nicklaus Cash, Goldman Sachs) and requests to "walk us through" scenarios (Albert Realini, Jefferies).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive on specific cost one-timers and hypotheticals in Q&A, including "you should remove for Q1" (CEO Goncalves) on the energy spike and "I don't like playing hypothetical scenarios" (CEO Goncalves) on POSCO alternatives.

  • Versus Q4 2025, management language shifted from "targeting signing a definitive agreement in the first half of 2026" (CEO Goncalves, prior call) to "a deal can be completed within this time frame or slightly a bit later" and "we are no longer in a hurry" (CEO Goncalves, current call).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current call emphasized near-term operational momentum—"order book is full" and pricing realization lag lengthening—whereas Q4 2025 centered on recovery setup and broader 2026 expectations such as "16.5 million- to 17 million-ton range" shipments and anticipated "unit cost... down another $10 per ton in 2026" (CFO Goncalves, prior call).

  • Canada discussion shifted from Q4’s view that Canadian restrictions "created positive momentum" (CEO Goncalves, prior call) to Q1’s statement that Canada remains "oversupplied" and "the one piece still missing is Canada" (CEO Goncalves), while CFO Goncalves quantified the gap: Canadian selling price at a "40% discount to U.S. pricing."

  • The slab contract narrative moved from Q4’s forward benefit framing ("$500 million" EBITDA order-of-magnitude cited in Q4 Q&A) to Q1’s clean-up conclusion: "175,000 tons" tail shipped and "it's over" (CEO Goncalves).

Risks and concerns

  • "The war activity in Iran has disrupted global freight lanes, driven up energy prices and destabilized metal supply chains" (CEO Goncalves), which management linked to higher energy and freight volatility.

  • "Our Q2 costs should tick up another $15 per ton higher" (CFO Goncalves) due to scrap/fuel pressures and outages, though he said costs should fall in the back half.

  • "One important milestone... is the renegotiation of our labor agreement with United Steelworkers" (CEO Goncalves), describing a need for "competitiveness, flexibility and long-term sustainability" in the agreement.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as the start of a year-long improvement path, pointing to a full order book, higher automotive pull, extended lead times, and a pricing realization lag that is expected to benefit Q2 and Q3. The quarter included $95 million of adjusted EBITDA and an $80 million EBITDA headwind from a weather-related energy spike, while management guided to Q2 selling price improvement of about $60 per ton, shipments above the Q1 level of just over 4.1 million tons, and a temporary $15 per ton cost step-up tied to outages and input costs before a back-half decline. The call also highlighted operational footprint actions in plate, ongoing POSCO talks with less urgency, a forthcoming AI partner announcement, and a targeted cadence for idled property sale proceeds within the company’s stated $425 million expectation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AnaptysBio rises after completing First Tracks spinoff
data.description:
• Shares of AnaptysBio (ANAB) climbed on Monday after completing the spinoff of its biopharma business, First Tracks Biotherapeutics (TRAXV), which is concurrently making its Nasdaq debut under the ticker symbol “TRAX.”
  • Following the transaction, Anaptys (ANAB) will become a royalty management company focused on its financial collaborations for cancer therapy Jemperli, developed with GSK (GSK), and skin disorder therapy imsidolimab, developed with Vanda (VNDA).
  • “We are now exclusively managing royalties from our out-licensed assets, Jemperli and imsidolimab, with streamlined operations requiring limited FTEs, minimal operating expenses, and providing a greater than 95% EBIT margin,” CEO Daniel Faga remarked.
  • Under the spinoff first announced in September, Anaptys (ANAN) shareholders received one share of First Tracks Bio (TRAX) common stock for each share of Anaptys (ANAB) common stock they held as of April 6.

data.title: WhatsApp testing premium subscription -- TechCrunch
data.description:
Meta Platform’s (META) WhatsApp is testing a subscription service that allows users to pay for cosmetic customizations, including ringtones, themes, and icons, similar to features offered on Instagram Plus and Snapchat+ (SNAP).  

“Premium features include expanded pinned chats, custom lists, new chat themes, and more. We’re starting with a small test to gather feedback and ensure we’re building something people find genuinely valuable,” a Meta spokesperson said to TechCrunch.

Paid messaging on WhatsApp has proven to be lucrative for the parent company, with Meta reporting a 54% increase in revenue from its family of apps in Q4 . Its WhatsApp Business API is expected to generate ~$2B in annual revenue this year, making it one of Meta’s fastest-growing segments.

data.title: Sibanye-Stillwater seeks E.U. concessions as it ramps up Finland lithium mine
data.description:
Sibanye-Stillwater (SBSW) said ‌Monday it is asking for concessions from the European Union to shield Europe's first large-scale lithium mining and processing venture from price volatility and unfair competition, Reuters reported.

Sibanye (SBSW) is advancing its ​Keliber lithium project in Finland in phases as it seeks to manage risk ​in a volatile pricing environment for the metal.

The company began mining ⁠lithium ore at the Syväjärvi open-cast mine in February and plans to commission ​a concentrator during Q3, producing spodumene concentrate at a rate ​of ~140K metric tons/year.

A decision on commissioning a refinery to produce ~15K metric tons/year of battery-grade lithium hydroxide will be taken in Q3 largely ​depends on ongoing talks with the European Union, including on a floor price, Sibanye's (SBSW) chief European advisor Mika Seitovirta told analysts at the company's Capital Day in Finland.

"Could we have some ​price protection mechanism for ​unfair competition, for ⁠instance? That is, of course, something that we need to have," Seitovirta said.

data.title: Wall Street slides as hopes of U.S.-Iran peace deal fade
data.description:
Wall Street traded lower on Monday as optimism over a potential U.S.-Iran peace deal faded, with escalating tensions in the Middle East weighing on investor sentiment and prompting a pullback in equities following last week’s rally.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.3%, while the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.5%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.1%. 

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point higher at 4.26%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.73%.

Iran will not participate in a second round of talks with the U.S., according to Iranian state media, which said Tehran objected to what it described as excessive and unrealistic demands from Washington.

U.S. President Donald Trump on Sunday accused Iran of violating a ceasefire agreement after Iranian forces fired upon ships in the Strait of Hormuz, including a French vessel and a British freighter.

In addition, activity through the Strait of Hormuz largely stopped after Iran reimposed control over it, just days before a fragile ceasefire with the U.S. is set to expire, according to Reuters.

Vice President JD Vance, along with the U.S. delegation, is expected to land in Islamabad, Pakistan, on Wednesday, CNN reported.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) are on the rise at $88, while Brent (CO1:COM) also rose to $95 per barrel.

As for stocks that were on the move, Marvell Technology (MRVL) climbed +4.4%, while shares of Intel (INTC) dropped -4.3%.
